meta.title: W638 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W638 Vito engine — M111, OM601, OM611. Real UK costs, common-rail injector advice, and honest reliability data for the first‑generation van. Get free quotes from vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes Vito W638 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W638 engine typically costs £1,200–£3,200 supply only, depending on engine — the OM601 diesel sits at £1,200–£2,200, while the OM611 CDI turbo-diesel ranges up to £3,200. Fitting adds £650–£1,000 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W638 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM601 naturally aspirated diesel (108 D / 110 D) — legendary Mercedes diesel durability, mechanical injection, no turbo, and nearly indestructible with basic maintenance. The OM611 CDI offers better performance but injector and EGR issues are common. The M111 petrol is a solid choice but timing chain wear is a known issue.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes Vito W638?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector failure on the OM611 CDI common-rail diesel, timing chain wear on the M111 petrol, injector pump wear on the OM601 diesel, and bodywork corrosion (rust) on all models — the bulkhead, door bottoms, wheel arches, and sills are the most common areas.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes Vito W638 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM601 diesel is one of the most reliable engines ever built. The OM611 CDI is reliable but injector maintenance is critical. The M111 petrol is solid but requires timing chain attention at higher mileages. The real reliability concern on the W638 is bodywork corrosion — the engine will often outlast the chassis.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W638 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a high-mileage OM611 CDI without documented injector history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A rusty W638 with any engine should be approached with caution. The OM601 diesel is the safest choice for commercial use.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W638 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rough running, loss of power, or black smoke (OM611 injector failure), rattling on cold start (M111 timing chain), hard starting or excessive smoke (OM601 injector pump wear), or a knocking bottom end (any engine) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes Vito W638 engine, or should I scrap the van?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the van's condition and value. Against a well‑kept £3,000 W638, a £2,200 OM601 recon is 73% — that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold. Against a tired £2,000 W638, the same recon is over 100% — walk away. For a commercial-use van with good bodywork, replacement may still make sense. Value the bodywork and service history first.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W638 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM601 diesel can exceed 300,000 miles with proper servicing — it's one of Mercedes' most durable engines. The OM611 CDI is capable of 200,000+ miles but injector replacement is a routine expense. The M111 petrol can exceed 200,000 miles but timing chain wear requires attention.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W638 Vito and the Viano / V-Class?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W638 Vito (this page, 1996–2003) is the commercial panel van, while the Viano / V‑Class is the passenger variant (people carrier) built on the same platform. Both share most engines but the passenger variant has additional soundproofing, trim, and passenger-oriented features. Costs vary slightly — see each model's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W638 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, and on the engine block itself (usually on the left side of the block). The engine code is also visible on a sticker in the engine bay. Tell us your variant and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes Vito W638 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation Vito — from the M111 petrol four‑cylinders to the OM601 naturally aspirated diesels and the OM611 CDI turbo‑diesel range that brought common‑rail refinement to the commercial van segment. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the engine that powered Europe's favourite mid‑sized van. Backed by 24,650+ real UK enquiries across all engines in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes Vito W638 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W638 Vito van specifically — including the petrol M111 range, the naturally aspirated OM601 diesel, and the common‑rail OM611 CDI turbo‑diesel range that arrived in 1999.

overview.intro: The W638 is the first‑generation Mercedes‑Benz Vito, a mid‑sized light commercial vehicle produced from November 1995 to 2003. Built at the Mercedes‑Benz España plant in Vitoria‑Gasteiz, Spain, it replaced the Mercedes‑Benz MB 100 and was available as a panel van, chassis cab, or multi‑purpose vehicle. The W638 featured a front‑wheel‑drive layout — a departure from the rear‑wheel‑drive MB 100 — and was powered by a range of petrol and diesel four‑cylinder engines. In 1997, the Vito was voted Van of the Year. The W638 was succeeded by the W639 in 2004.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: W638 (Mercedes‑Benz first‑generation Vito) • Predecessor: Mercedes‑Benz MB 100 • Successor: W639 (second‑generation Vito) • Sibling body styles in this generation: Viano / V‑Class (passenger variant)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W638 Vito generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM601 diesel and M111 petrol variants as these vans enter their third decade of commercial service.

economicsRule.title: The W638 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,200 OM601 recon against a £3,000 W638 sits at 73% — that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold. Against a tired £2,000 W638, the same recon is over 100% — walk away. A £3,200 OM611 recon against a well‑kept £4,500 example is 71% — borderline. Value the van and its service history first. Our 70% Mercedes threshold applies strictly to W638s with commercial use and high mileage.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W638 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM601 (108 D / 110 D) — cheap injector pump maintenance, proven mechanical diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W638 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM611 (112 CDI) — injector failure and EGR issues add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W638 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM601 (110 D) — the most durable engine in the range.

problems.title: OM611 Injector Failure (108/110/112 CDI)
problems.rootCause: The OM611 common‑rail diesel injectors can fail, causing rough running, loss of power, increased fuel consumption, and black smoke. Injector failure is the single biggest maintenance cost on CDI‑equipped W638 vans.
problems.recommendation: If your CDI‑equipped W638 develops rough running, loss of power, or excessive black smoke, get the injectors tested immediately — a £400–£1,000 injector replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £1,500–£2,800 replacement that follows a failed injector.

cta.label: Compare OM611 engine prices for your W638 112 CDI →

problems.title: M111 Timing Chain Wear (113, V 230)
problems.rootCause: The M111 petrol engine's timing chain can stretch and wear over time, causing rattling on start‑up and, in extreme cases, timing failure. Regular oil changes with the correct specification are essential.
problems.recommendation: If your petrol W638 develops a rattling noise on cold start, get the timing chain inspected immediately — a £300–£800 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £1,200–£2,200 replacement that follows a jumped chain.

cta.label: Compare M111 engine prices for your W638 113 →

problems.title: OM601 Injector Pump Wear (108 D, 110 D)
problems.rootCause: The mechanical injection pump on OM601 engines wears over time, causing hard starting, reduced power, and increased smoke. The engine is otherwise nearly indestructible.
problems.recommendation: If your 108 D or 110 D develops hard starting or excessive smoke, get the injection system inspected — a £300–£800 pump rebuild is far cheaper than the £1,200–£2,200 replacement that follows a failed pump.

cta.label: Compare OM601 engine prices for your W638 110 D →

problems.title: W638 Rust & Bodywork Corrosion
problems.rootCause: The W638's body panels and chassis are prone to corrosion — the bulkhead, door bottoms, wheel arches, and sills are the most common areas. This is the single biggest factor affecting W638 viability.
problems.recommendation: Before spending any money on a W638 engine, get the bodywork and chassis thoroughly inspected — a £500–£1,500 rust repair is far cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 restoration that follows a neglected bulkhead or chassis failure.

replacementCosts.h2: W638 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline figures — W638 van shares engine architecture with other Mercedes vans of the era, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W638 Vito van only. Viano / V‑Class passenger variants share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages.
